default.hero: A year-by-year guide connecting courses, internships, and technical skill-building for your path in Computer Science.
default.highlight: This roadmap is a starting point — a guide to the classes, technical skills, and recruiting timelines worth thinking about as you navigate a career in software engineering, AI, systems, or data.

programs.text: Freshman and sophomore engineering exposure programs
Faculty research and independent study opportunities

resources: LeetCode
NeetCode
System Design Primer
Colby CS research opportunities

freshman.hero: Freshman year is about starting the CS sequence and learning the foundations of programming and computational thinking.
freshman.highlight: Best use of freshman year: complete CS15X early, build strong programming fundamentals, and start exploring different areas of computer science such as software engineering, AI, systems, and data.
classes: MA 125 / MA 130 / MA 160 (math foundation depending on placement)
resources: Learn Python or Java deeply
Attend hackathons or CS club events
Begin simple programming projects
Focus on strong study habits early

sophomore.hero: Sophomore year introduces the core technical foundations of the CS major.
sophomore.highlight: Best use of sophomore year: complete the systems and data structures courses while starting technical interview preparation and applying for sophomore engineering programs.
classes: MA 2XX math course (Linear Algebra, Probability, or Discrete Math)
resources: Start regular LeetCode practice
Learn Git and collaborative development
Build at least two meaningful personal projects
Start tracking internship recruiting timelines

junior.hero: Junior year is when advanced CS coursework connects directly with internship recruiting.
junior.highlight: Best use of junior year: take advanced CS courses, strengthen technical interview preparation, and secure a summer software engineering internship.
classes: CS 3XX electives (AI, Machine Learning, Databases, Distributed Systems, Networks)
resources: Junior summer internship is the most important for full-time conversion
Practice algorithms and system design interviews
Build larger technical projects
Use networking and alumni outreach more seriously

senior.hero: Senior year focuses on advanced electives and converting internship experience into full-time opportunities.
senior.highlight: Best use of senior year: complete advanced CS electives, refine your technical portfolio, and use fall recruiting to secure a full-time engineering role.
classes: CS 3XX theory course (if not completed earlier)
resources: Finalize portfolio and GitHub projects
Practice system design interviews
Leverage internship experience for full-time roles
Use alumni networking and referrals

default.hero: A year-by-year guide connecting courses, opportunities, and practical next steps for your path in Economics.
default.highlight: This roadmap is a starting point — a guide to the classes, programs, and resources worth thinking about as you navigate a career in Economics.
resources: Wall Street Prep
Paving the Road
Mergers and Acquisitions class with Mike McHale

freshman.hero: Freshman year is mainly about getting the intro sequence started and beginning to understand what the major looks like.
freshman.highlight: Best use of freshman year: complete EC133 and EC134, start building your foundation early, and get familiar with finance, policy, consulting, and research paths before recruiting ramps up later.
resources: Get the intro sequence moving early
Start learning what finance, consulting, policy, and quant paths look like
Use first-year events and networking to explore without overcommitting
Keep options open by building strong study habits from the start

sophomore.hero: Sophomore year is where the major becomes more structured — theory, methods, and math become much more important.
sophomore.highlight: Best use of sophomore year: finish the core theory and methods work so junior year can focus on stronger electives, technical development, and internship preparation.
classes: PS 214: Research Methods or EC 293: Econ Research Methods
Any Math class between MA 125 to MA 160
resources: Become more intentional about recruiting direction
Methods and math support upper-level econ and more technical roles
Build spreadsheet, data, and interview skills alongside coursework
Start keeping a clear timeline for sophomore and junior recruiting windows

junior.hero: Junior year is usually the most important year for connecting coursework to internships, recruiting, and upper-level economics work.
junior.highlight: Best use of junior year: pair upper-level econ classes with serious internship prep, stronger technical skill building, and a more focused recruiting strategy.
resources: Junior summer is often the most important internship summer
Econometrics makes your quantitative background more usable
Use networking and alumni outreach more seriously this year
Tighten your resume and know what roles you are targeting

senior.hero: Senior year should be about finishing strong, completing the seminar requirement, and turning your experience into a clear next step.
senior.highlight: Best use of senior year: finish upper-level work well, complete the seminar, and use the fall to convert your experience into full-time opportunities or a stronger post-grad plan.

programs.text: Focus on full-time recruiting and late-cycle opportunities
Use seminar and upper-level work to sharpen your interests

resources: Wall Street Prep
Paving the Road
Mergers and Acquisitions class with Mike McHale
Senior year is about finishing the major well and making your next move concrete
